sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A biomechanics lab modeled elastic resistance bands as springs. Force was measured while stretching one band by 0.05, 0.10, and 0.15 m. Forces were 5, 10, and 15 N, showing approximately Hookean behavior. Students calculated the spring constant and work done to stretch the band.
questions.stem: What is the spring constant of the band?

explanations.B: Correct. Hooke’s law gives F = kx. Using F = 10 N at x = 0.10 m gives k = 10/0.10 = 100 N/m, consistent with the other data points.
explanations.A: 10 N/m would produce only 1 N at 0.10 m.
explanations.C: 50 N/m would produce 5 N at 0.10 m, not 10 N.
explanations.D: 1000 N/m would produce 100 N at 0.10 m.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Force required to stretch a resistance band by different distances.

questions.stem: How much work is required to stretch the band from 0 to 0.10 m?

explanations.C: Correct. Work stored in a spring is 1/2kx^2. With k = 100 N/m and x = 0.10 m, W = 0.5 × 100 × 0.01 = 0.50 J.
explanations.A: 1.0 J would omit the one-half factor in spring energy.
explanations.B: 5.0 J is too large by a factor of 10.
explanations.D: 10 J is the force at 0.10 m, not the work.

questions.stem: The area under the force-stretch curve represents:

explanations.A: Correct. Work is the integral of force over displacement. On a force-versus-stretch graph, the area under the curve is the work required to stretch the band.
explanations.B: Pressure is force per area, not area under a force-distance graph.
explanations.C: Electric potential is not what this mechanics graph measures.
explanations.D: Mass density is mass per volume.

questions.stem: Why is the work not simply Fmax × x?

choices.D: Force increases from zero to Fmax during the stretch
choices.B: The band has no elastic energy

explanations.D: Correct. For a Hookean spring, force grows linearly with extension. The average force from 0 to x is Fmax/2, so work is 1/2Fmaxx rather than Fmaxx.
explanations.A: Force is directly related to displacement in Hooke’s law.
explanations.B: Elastic energy is stored in the stretched band.

questions.stem: If two identical bands are used in parallel, the effective spring constant would:

explanations.C: Correct. Springs in parallel share the displacement while their forces add. Two identical bands each contribute kx force, giving total force 2kx and an effective spring constant of 2k.
explanations.A: Halving is typical of identical springs in series, not parallel.
explanations.B: The bands would still resist stretching, so the constant is not zero.
explanations.D: Adding a second parallel band increases resistance.

questions.passage: A 70 kg subject pushed a weighted sled up a 5.0 m ramp that rose 1.0 m vertically. The sled-subject system gained 700 J of gravitational potential energy, while motion sensors estimated that 200 J of energy was dissipated by friction. The subject completed the push in 4.0 s. Students compared work, power, and efficiency.
questions.stem: What total mechanical work input is estimated from the energy accounting?

explanations.B: Correct. The subject must supply energy for both the gravitational potential energy gain and frictional losses. Adding 700 J and 200 J gives 900 J total work input.
explanations.A: 700 J accounts only for useful gravitational potential energy.
explanations.C: 200 J accounts only for dissipated energy.
explanations.D: 500 J would incorrectly subtract friction from the energy gain.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Energy changes during a sled push up an incline.

questions.stem: What average power was produced over 4.0 s?

explanations.D: Correct. Average power is work divided by time. Using the total input work, P = 900 J / 4.0 s = 225 W.
explanations.A: 175 W would use only the 700 J potential energy gain and ignore friction.
explanations.B: 50 W would use only the dissipated energy.
explanations.C: 3600 W incorrectly multiplies work by time instead of dividing.

questions.stem: Which energy term represents useful mechanical output for raising the sled?

choices.D: Zero because the ramp is inclined

explanations.A: Correct. The useful output for moving the system upward is the increase in gravitational potential energy, mgh. Frictional dissipation is energy input that does not contribute to height gain.
explanations.B: Frictional energy is lost to thermal processes and is not useful output.
explanations.C: Thermal loss is a cost, not the useful lifting output.
explanations.D: An inclined ramp still raises the system vertically, so potential energy changes.

questions.stem: What is the approximate mechanical efficiency?

explanations.B: Correct. Efficiency is useful output divided by total input. The useful gain is 700 J and total input is 900 J, so efficiency is about 0.78, or 78%.
explanations.A: 200/900 is the fraction dissipated, not useful efficiency.
explanations.C: Efficiency above 100% would imply more useful output than input, which is not the case.
explanations.D: Energy conservation does not mean all input energy becomes useful output.

questions.stem: Why does using a ramp reduce required force compared with lifting straight up?

choices.C: The same height gain is spread over a longer distance
choices.D: Work is always zero on an incline

explanations.C: Correct. A ramp increases the distance over which the upward height change occurs, reducing the force needed along the ramp in an ideal case. With friction, total work can increase, but force can still be lower.
explanations.A: Gravity still acts on the sled-subject system.
explanations.B: Mass does not become zero on a ramp.
explanations.D: Work is not zero because energy changes and friction dissipates energy.
